summary: George Hotz argues that AI will create jobs, not destroy them, drawing a direct parallel between AI and immigration. He invokes Jevons paradox to explain that making things more efficient leads to more total usage, not less. He dismisses concerns about AI displacing workers as protectionism, comparing it to anti-immigration sentiment. His conclusion is that the overall economic pie will grow and there will be more work than ever before.
key_points: AI and immigration are fundamentally the same economic phenomenon: new productive capacity entering the system creates more jobs, not fewer
Jevons paradox applies to AI — making things more efficient increases total demand rather than reducing it
Protectionism against AI displacement is for losers, just like protectionism against immigrant labor
Inequality should be addressed by design rather than redistribution, while focusing on growing the overall pie
money_quotes: AI and immigration are fundamentally the same. There's new people showing up, and hopefully everyone understands how and why immigration creates jobs.
It's classic Jevons paradox that if we make something more efficient, we end up using more of it.
If you make the site 10% faster, people spend 5% more total time on it.
AI will outcompete some humans at some jobs. But protectionism is for losers.
The important thing is that the overall pie grows, and inequality stays somewhat in check, not by redistribution but by design.
There will be more to do than ever before.
key_insight: AI, like immigration, expands the economic pie through Jevons paradox — efficiency gains create more demand and more jobs, not fewer.
